nativeRange.habitat: Sandy areas in clear lagoons and seaward reefs; pairs typically share shallow burrows beneath rubble.

decorPreferences: live rock with shallow rubble for burrow refuge

diet.notes: Obligate sand-sifter that feeds on copepods, amphipods and other microfauna picked from mouthfuls of sand. Generally advised to offer frequent small portions of mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, live blackworms and quality carnivore preparations, ideally placed near the burrow with a feeding stick. Has been reported to emaciate in undersized or biologically immature systems lacking a mature sand-bed microfauna population; a refugium or routine pod top-ups are commonly recommended.

avoidWith: conspecifics (other Valenciennea) unless a bonded pair

summary: The Diamond Watchman Goby is a peaceful, sand-sifting species from Indo-Pacific reefs, widely regarded as useful for aerating substrate. Generally considered reef-safe with caution, it requires a deep, mature sand bed with an established microfauna population to thrive.
careNotes: The Diamond Watchman Goby is a bottom-dwelling sand-sifter that spends most of the day taking mouthfuls of substrate and sieving out small invertebrates. A fine-grain sand bed of at least 5 cm — commonly around 2.5 to 3 inches of sugar-fine sand — is generally advised so the fish can both feed and excavate the shallow burrows it uses as refuge. Burrow construction often involves piling sand against adjacent rockwork and lower corals, which has been reported to bury or irritate sessile invertebrates; the species is therefore best regarded as reef-safe with caution rather than unconditionally reef-safe. Tankmates are generally tolerated well, but conspecifics (other members of the same or closely related species) are typically fought unless introduced as an established bonded pair; the Diamond Watchman Goby is monogamous in the wild, and pairs share a single burrow. A tightly sealed lid is essential, as these gobies are well documented as strong jumpers. The most common captive failure is slow starvation in tanks with insufficient microfauna — a mature system with a productive sand bed, live rock, or a connected refugium is strongly recommended, and target-feeding with a feeding stick is often required until the fish is reliably accepting prepared foods. A tank of at least 150 L (approximately 40 US gallons) with a long footprint is generally advised for an adult, which can reach 13 to 18 cm and remains active across the bottom throughout the day.
breedingNotes: Pairs are monogamous and have been spawned in captivity occasionally; eggs are deposited inside the shared burrow and guarded by the parents. Rearing larvae is reported to be difficult due to small first-feeding mouths requiring rotifers and copepod nauplii, and captive breeding is not routine in the hobby.

additional.notes: Taxonomy (Gobiidae/Gobiiformes), wild max size (20 cm SL), depth range (2-84 m, usually 10-30 m), Indo-Pacific distribution, monogamous pair biology. Swapped in for designated secondary because Reef2Reef wiki returned 403.

additional.name: Fish Laboratory
additional.notes: Cross-check: tank size 30 gal, 6 in aquarium / 7 in wild adult size, sand depth 2.5-3 in sugar-fine sand, microfauna-dependence feeding caveat, high jumping/escape risk, ~5 yr lifespan.

additional.name: Aquarium Source
additional.notes: Cross-check: 5-7 in adult size, 30 gal minimum on a long footprint (30 x 12 in), 72-78 F / pH 8.1-8.5 / SG 1.020-1.025 / 8-12 dKH, 3 in substrate minimum, monogamous pair-for-life behavior, 5-8 yr lifespan.

fish.breedingNotes: Monogamous pair-spawners; eggs guarded in the shared burrow but larval rearing on rotifers/copepod nauplii is reported as challenging.
